narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid background, we've carefully reviewed your law school application using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, and unfortunately, it led to a rejection. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 1, lower-middle family income, an LSAT score of 35, an undergraduate GPA of 2.50, and plans to attend full-time—credentials that show determination but also some areas where the model sees risks. The model estimates a 41% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough for the admissions team to view it as too uncertain for approval in this competitive process. The key driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking at the bottom of your class, as it stood out as the strongest signal of potential struggles ahead compared to most students who rank much higher on average. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 35 had the biggest favorable influence, performing closer to the typical strong applicant's level and pulling the prediction toward success. Your lower-middle family income also helped somewhat, nudging things positively since students from similar backgrounds often succeed with grit like yours. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 provided a modest boost too, though it's below the average of stronger applicants. Less influential but still in your favor was your full-time study plan, aligning with the vast majority of successful students, while your Year 1 decile added a small push toward concern. Overall, compared to typical admitted students—who boast higher deciles around 5-6, LSATs near 37, and GPAs over 3.2—your lower Year 3 performance was the standout gap that tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant with clear drive; it's just the model's data-driven read on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your recent academic deciles through targeted prep or additional coursework, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that align more closely with top performers—these steps have turned around many situations like yours. We're rooting for your success.
